---
title: "Lists"
description: "Store, access, slice, update, copy, and loop over ordered mutable collections."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## The problem this lesson solves

A single variable can store one value. Real programs often need a group of
values: many scores, many names, many tasks, many measurements. A **list** stores
multiple values in one ordered container.

Lists are one of the first data structures worth mastering because they combine
several core ideas:

- order: items have positions;
- mutability: the list can change;
- iteration: loops can visit each item;
- indexing and slicing: code can pick one item or a smaller list.

## How lists store ordered items

::: {.concept-diagram}
A list is one object that stores items in order. Names can point to that same
list, or to a separate copy.

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| eval: true
flowchart LR
  tasks["name<br/>tasks"] --> list["list object<br/>index 0: 'predict'<br/>index 1: 'practice'<br/>index 2: 'reflect'"]
  alias["name<br/>alias"] --> list
  copy["name<br/>copy"] --> copylist["separate list object<br/>index 0: 'predict'<br/>index 1: 'practice'"]
  list --> slice["slice tasks[1:3]<br/>new list with indexes 1 and 2"]
```

This diagram explains why aliasing can surprise beginners: two names can point to
one mutable list object.
:::

## 1. Creating, reading, and changing lists

A list is written with square brackets. Items are separated by commas.

```python
scores = [10, 8, 9]
names = ["Ada", "Grace", "Katherine"]
```

A list can hold any type of value, but beginner programs are usually easier to
understand when one list contains one kind of thing.

### First example

Predict the final printed list before running the code.

```python
tasks = ["read", "practice"]
tasks.append("reflect")
tasks[0] = "predict"
print(tasks)
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `tasks = ["read", "practice"]`
   - Python creates a list with two strings.
   - Index `0` contains `"read"`; index `1` contains `"practice"`.
2. `tasks.append("reflect")`
   - `.append(...)` adds a new item to the end of the existing list.
   - The list is now `["read", "practice", "reflect"]`.
3. `tasks[0] = "predict"`
   - Index `0` is replaced.
   - The list is now `["predict", "practice", "reflect"]`.
4. `print(tasks)`
   - Python displays the whole list.

### Indexes and length

```python
colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]
print(colors[0])      # red
print(colors[2])      # blue
print(len(colors))    # 3
```

`len(colors)` is `3`, but the last index is `2`. This is a common beginner
surprise: indexes start at `0`, so a list of length `3` has indexes `0`, `1`,
and `2`.

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
**Mistake: reaching past the end.**

```python
colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]
print(colors[3])
```

This raises `IndexError`. The last valid index is `len(colors) - 1`, which is
`2` here.
:::

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "lists-basics-checkpoint",
  "title": "List basics checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "first-index",
      "prompt": "What is the first valid index of a Python list?",
      "options": [
        "0",
        "1",
        "-0",
        "len(list)"
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Python indexes start at 0."
    },
    {
      "id": "append-effect",
      "prompt": "What does tasks.append(\"reflect\") do?",
      "options": [
        "Adds \"reflect\" to the end of the existing list.",
        "Creates a new string named tasks.",
        "Deletes the first item.",
        "Sorts the list alphabetically."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "append mutates the list by adding one item at the end."
    },
    {
      "id": "last-index",
      "prompt": "If len(colors) is 3, what is the last valid positive index?",
      "options": [
        "3",
        "2",
        "1",
        "0"
      ],
      "answer_index": 1,
      "explanation": "A length-3 list has indexes 0, 1, and 2."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. List methods and loops

A **method** is an action attached to a value. List methods change or inspect a
list.

| Method | Meaning | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `.append(item)` | add item to the end | `tasks.append("review")` |
| `.remove(item)` | remove first matching item | `tasks.remove("read")` |
| `.pop()` | remove and return the last item | `last = tasks.pop()` |
| `.sort()` | sort the list in place | `scores.sort()` |

### First example

```python
scores = [70, 90, 80]
scores.append(100)
scores.sort()

for score in scores:
    print(score)
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `scores = [70, 90, 80]` creates a list.
2. `scores.append(100)` changes that same list by adding `100` at the end.
3. `scores.sort()` changes the list order to `[70, 80, 90, 100]`.
4. `for score in scores:` visits each score one at a time.
5. The indented `print(score)` runs once per score.

### Method return values

Some list methods change the list and return `None`. This mistake is common:

```python
scores = [70, 90, 80]
sorted_scores = scores.sort()
print(sorted_scores)
```

This prints `None` because `.sort()` changes `scores` in place. If you want a
new sorted list, use `sorted(...)`:

```python
scores = [70, 90, 80]
sorted_scores = sorted(scores)
print(sorted_scores)
```

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "lists-methods-loops-checkpoint",
  "title": "List methods and loops checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "sort-return",
      "prompt": "What does scores.sort() return?",
      "options": [
        "None",
        "A new sorted list",
        "The largest score",
        "A string"
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "list.sort() changes the list in place and returns None."
    },
    {
      "id": "loop-over-list",
      "prompt": "What does for score in scores: do?",
      "options": [
        "Runs the indented block once for each score.",
        "Runs the block only for the first score.",
        "Deletes each score.",
        "Creates a dictionary."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "A for loop visits each list item in order."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 3. Slicing, copying, and aliasing

A **slice** creates a smaller list from part of another list.

```python
letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d"]
print(letters[1:3])
```

The result is `["b", "c"]`. The start index `1` is included. The stop index `3`
is not included.

### Why stop is not included

Think of the slice as cutting between items:

```text
indexes:    0     1     2     3
items:    "a"   "b"   "c"   "d"
cut:     |     |     |     |     |
              start       stop
```

`letters[1:3]` starts at index `1` and stops before index `3`.

### Aliasing: two names, one list

```python
original = ["read", "practice"]
alias = original
alias.append("reflect")
print(original)
```

This prints `["read", "practice", "reflect"]`. `alias` and `original` are two
names for the same list.

If you need a separate list, copy it:

```python
original = ["read", "practice"]
copy = original.copy()
copy.append("reflect")
print(original)
print(copy)
```

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
**Mistake: expecting assignment to copy a list.**

`alias = original` does not copy the list. It adds another name for the same list
object. Use `.copy()` or a slice `original[:]` for a shallow copy.
:::

### Practice

In Colab, create a shopping list and practice one operation at a time:

```python
shopping = ["rice", "beans", "apples"]
shopping.append("tea")
shopping.remove("beans")
print(shopping[:2])
```

Before each line, predict the exact list.

### Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "lists-copying-checkpoint",
  "title": "Slicing and copying checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "slice-stop",
      "prompt": "What does letters[1:3] include?",
      "options": [
        "Indexes 1 and 2, but not 3.",
        "Indexes 1, 2, and 3.",
        "Only index 3.",
        "All indexes except 1 and 3."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The stop index in a slice is excluded."
    },
    {
      "id": "alias-effect",
      "prompt": "What does alias = original do for a list?",
      "options": [
        "It creates another name for the same list.",
        "It always makes a deep copy.",
        "It freezes the original list.",
        "It removes all items."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Assignment does not copy a list object; it creates another reference to it."
    },
    {
      "id": "copy-method",
      "prompt": "Which expression makes a shallow copy of a list?",
      "options": [
        "original.copy()",
        "alias = original",
        "len(original)",
        "original.append()"
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": ".copy() creates a separate shallow copy of the list."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- Lists store ordered collections of values.
- Indexes start at `0`; the last index is `len(items) - 1`.
- Lists are mutable: methods and item assignment can change them.
- A `for` loop can process each list item.
- Slices use `start:stop`, where stop is not included.
- Assignment creates an alias, not a copy.
:::

## References

- Python Tutorial: lists: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/introduction.html#lists
- Python Tutorial: more on lists: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#more-on-lists
